attributes.stem:
A 63-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a two-week history of gum bleeding and easy bruising. She also reports increasing fatigue over the past three months and intermittent mild epistaxis. She has a medical history of hypertension. Her regular medications are amlodipine and an angiotensin receptor blocker. She works as an accountant and does not drink alcohol or use recreational drugs.

On examination, she appears well with a pulse rate of 82/min, blood pressure 138/82 mmHg, respiratory rate 15/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. There is no lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly.

Investigations reveal:

  • Haemoglobin: 100 g/L (115-155)
  • White cell count: 2.8 × 109/L (3.6-11.0)
  • Platelets: 28 × 109/L (140-400)
  • Lactate dehydrogenase: 700 U/L (240-480)
  • Prothrombin time: 18 s (11-15)
  • Activated partial thromboplastin time: 45 s (25-38)
  • Fibrinogen: 0.8 g/L (2.0-4.0)
  • D-dimer: 25 mg/L FEU (<0.5)

Peripheral blood film shows many blasts and atypical promyelocytes, some with many Auer rods.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute promyelocytic leukaemia with disseminated intravascular coagulation
attributes.explanation:
Bleeding, bruising and mucosal haemorrhage in the context of pancytopenia and a coagulopathy should raise concern for a marrow process complicated by disseminated intravascular coagulation. The blood film description of abnormal promyelocytes with Auer rods is highly suggestive of acute promyelocytic leukaemia, a subtype of acute myeloid leukaemia associated with the PML::RARA fusion. Disseminated intravascular coagulation is common at presentation, driving early haemorrhagic mortality.

Chronic myeloid leukaemia with hyperleucocytosis more often presents with marked leucocytosis and splenomegaly, sometimes with leucostasis symptoms; disseminated intravascular coagulation at presentation is less characteristic, and a predominance of abnormal promyelocytes with Auer rod bundles is not expected.

Acute myeloid leukaemia with hyperleucocytosis typically presents with a markedly elevated white cell count (>100 × 109/L) and symptoms of leucostasis such as dyspnoea, confusion, or visual changes; while disseminated intravascular coagulation can occur, the specific finding of abnormal promyelocytes with multiple Auer rods points to acute promyelocytic leukaemia rather than other acute myeloid leukaemia subtypes.

Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura presents with thrombocytopenia and microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia (schistocytes), often with neurological or renal features; coagulation tests are typically within reference intervals and blasts or abnormal promyelocytes are not seen, which contrasts with the disseminated intravascular coagulation and promyelocytes here.

Chronic lymphocytic leukaemia typically presents with lymphocytosis rather than pancytopenia, often with lymphadenopathy and splenomegaly; the blood film shows mature small lymphocytes with smudge cells rather than blasts or promyelocytes, and disseminated intravascular coagulation at presentation is not characteristic.

Acute promyelocytic leukaemia with disseminated intravascular coagulation best explains the constellation of findings: cytopenias, raised lactate dehydrogenase, prolonged prothrombin time and activated partial thromboplastin time, very low fibrinogen, markedly elevated D-dimer, and a film rich in atypical promyelocytes with Auer rods. Recognising this pattern is crucial because prompt differentiation therapy rapidly reverses the coagulopathy.
learningPoints: Acute promyelocytic leukaemia commonly presents with pancytopenia and disseminated intravascular coagulation causing mucocutaneous bleeding.
Recognition of abnormal promyelocytes with Auer rods on blood film alongside disseminated intravascular coagulation should prompt immediate consideration of acute promyelocytic leukaemia.
references: Ryan MM. Acute Promyelocytic Leukemia: A Summary. J Adv Pract Oncol. 2018 Mar;9(2):178-187.
Yilmaz M, Kantarjian H, Ravandi F. Acute promyelocytic leukemia current treatment algorithms. Blood Cancer J. 2021 Jun 30;11(6):1-9.
